reason: Shows that some criminals desire execution, undermining the premise that the death penalty is a threat to criminals
The study’s claim of widespread trial errors attacks the premise that deserved punishment can be justly applied.
Sanctioning killing as pay‑back directly contradicts the claim that deserved punishment morally restores order.
Pro36 suggests death may not be sufficient deterrent, challenging the premise that deserved punishment fully compensates for harm.
pro36 questions the moral adequacy of death as final punishment, challenging pro15's premise that deserved punishment restores moral order
con36 shows executions can be wrongful, undermining the premise that punishment is deserved and morally protective
con189 argues no remedy after a mistaken execution, challenging the moral justification of pro15
con27 provides evidence of wrongful convictions, weakening pro15’s claim of deserved punishment
con174 highlights socioeconomic bias, attacking the fairness premise behind pro15’s deserved‑punishment claim
con190 questions the fairness of executing individuals with brain defects, undermining the moral legitimacy of pro15’s argument
Con36 highlights the risk of executing innocent people, challenging the premise that the punished individual is truly the wrongdoer.
Con189 points out the irreversibility of execution, undermining the premise that deserved punishment can be correctly applied.
Con27 provides evidence of wrongful convictions, attacking the premise that the punished person is the actual wrongdoer.
Con174 argues that capital punishment disproportionately affects the poor, challenging the fairness premise of deserved punishment.
Con190 questions the fairness of executing individuals with brain defects, undermining the premise that punishment is based solely on harm caused.
Con229 cites systematic trial errors, attacking the premise that the convicted are correctly identified as wrongdoers.
Con140 frames executions as sanctioned pay‑back killing, challenging the moral premise that such punishment is a just equivalent to harm.
Con36 argues the death penalty is unacceptable because innocent people may be executed, contradicting pro4’s claim of acceptability.
Con189 states that wrongful execution makes the penalty irrevocable, opposing pro4’s stance that it is an acceptable measure.
Con27 highlights frequent wrongful convictions, directly challenging the acceptability asserted in pro4.
Con174 frames capital punishment as unjust punishment of the poor, contradicting pro4’s claim of its acceptability.
Con190 questions the fairness of executing individuals with brain defects, opposing the notion that death penalty is appropriate.
Con229 cites systematic trial errors, undermining the legitimacy of the death penalty endorsed by pro4.
Con140 argues that executions legitimize revenge killing, directly conflicting with pro4’s claim of acceptability.
B argues the death penalty is insufficient and draconian, contradicting A’s justification that it prevents repeat offenses.
B highlights the risk of executing innocent people, directly opposing A’s justification of the death penalty.
B points out the irreversibility of wrongful executions, contradicting A’s claim that death penalty is warranted.
B shows that some executed were innocent, challenging the premise that all those killed are repeat offenders.
B points out serious errors in capital trials, attacking the premise that the convicted repeat offenders are correctly identified.
B highlights the risk of executing innocent people, challenging the premise that the system’s threat is justified.
The fairness objection about brain defects attacks the premise that the offender fully deserves retributive death.
The claim of widespread trial errors undermines the premise that retributive executions are reliably just.
One asserts execution honors victims, the other asserts it merely sanctions killing, making the conclusions mutually exclusive.
The risk of executing the innocent challenges the premise that retribution is a morally sound response.
The irreversibility of execution undermines the premise that retributive justice can be safely applied.
Evidence of wrongful convictions attacks the premise that death sentences are correctly assigned.
The claim that capital punishment disproportionately harms the poor undermines the fairness premise of retributive justice.
Questioning the fairness of killing individuals with brain defects attacks the premise that they fully merit retributive death.
B shows that many capital trials have serious errors, challenging the premise that capital punishment is applied reliably enough to serve as an effective threat.
B shows that innocent people can be executed, challenging A's premise that death penalty reliably removes only guilty perpetrators.
B shows that some executed were innocent, challenging A's premise that the death penalty only targets guilty perpetrators.
B shows A's conclusion (death penalty justified) doesn't follow even if its premise (prevents future tragedy) holds, by raising fairness concerns
A's conclusion that retribution justifies the death penalty directly contradicts B's conclusion that the death penalty should be rejected because most capital trials contain serious errors.
A claims executions are justified to prevent future harm, while B claims they morally sanction killing, making the conclusions mutually exclusive.
